title: Conservation of Energy
summary: The unit's master tool. Energy changes form but never vanishes — track it through ramps, springs, and heat to crack problems kinematics can't touch.

steps.title: Energy is never lost
steps.body:
Energy doesn't appear or disappear — it just **changes form**. A roller coaster at the top of the hill is all **potential energy**; at the bottom it's all **kinetic**. The total never changed.

This is the most powerful idea in the whole unit. Once you can track energy as it shifts between forms, you can solve problems that would be a nightmare with force-and-motion equations alone.

steps.title: The rule
steps.body:
When only **conservative** forces (gravity, springs) act, **mechanical energy is conserved** — KE and PE just trade back and forth and their sum holds fixed.

Slide the ball down the frictionless ramp below. Watch potential energy pour into kinetic energy while the **total bar never moves**.

steps.prompt: A ball is released from rest at the top of a **frictionless** ramp, where it has **PE_g = 30 J** (and KE = 0). Drag the **total mechanical energy** it has at the bottom.
hints: With no friction, mechanical energy is conserved — the total can't grow or shrink.
The energy just changes form (PE → KE). The total at the bottom equals the total at the top.
steps.solution: On a frictionless ramp, mechanical energy is conserved: KE + PE stays constant. At the top it's all potential (30 J); at the bottom it's all kinetic — but the total never changed. So the total mechanical energy at the bottom is **30 J**.

steps.title: Where v = √(2gh) comes from
steps.body:
Before you play with it, watch the formula build itself. Drop from rest down a frictionless track and **every** joule of potential energy at the top becomes kinetic energy at the bottom — so **mgh = ½mv²**.

The **same mass** sits on both sides, so it cancels completely. Two tidy algebra steps later, the speed at the bottom falls out: v = √(2gh).

steps.title: How fast at the bottom?
steps.body:
That's the move that makes conservation so useful: v = √(2gh). The speed at the bottom depends only on the **height** you fell — never on how heavy you are or what shape the track is.

In the explorer below, set the **release height** and read the speed. Then drag the **mass** slider — and watch the needle refuse to move.

steps.prompt: Drag the **release height** of a frictionless drop and watch the speed at the bottom respond. The speed grows — but not as fast as the height does.
steps.task: **Quadruple** the height — drag it from 2.5 m up to 10 m, then hit **Drop it** and watch the speed build. Then compare the two speed bars.
steps.solution: Speed at the bottom is v = √(2gh), so v depends on the **square root** of height. Quadrupling the height (2.5 m → 10 m) only **doubles** the speed (7.0 m/s → 14 m/s), because √4 = 2. Height has to climb fast to buy you a little extra speed — the same square-root relationship, run in reverse, is why KE depends on v².

steps.title: Watch a conservation problem worked
steps.prompt: A **2.0 kg** cart is released from rest **2.5 m** up a **frictionless** track. How fast is it moving at the bottom? (g = 9.8)
steps: All the potential energy at the top becomes kinetic at the bottom: mgh = ½mv².
The mass is on both sides, so it cancels: gh = ½v², i.e. v = √(2gh).
steps.takeaway: Notice we never needed the mass — v = √(2gh) is the same for a feather or a fridge dropped the same height.

steps.prompt: Your turn — setup's done. A **3.0 kg** ball drops on a frictionless track and arrives at the bottom with **96 J** of kinetic energy. How fast is it going?
shownSteps: All the energy is kinetic at the bottom: ½mv² = 96
steps.hint: v² = 64, so v is the square root of 64.

steps.title: The path doesn't matter either
steps.body:
Because gravity is conservative, only the **vertical drop** decides the energy — not the route taken to get down.

A steep straight slide and a long curvy one that start and end at the same heights deliver the **same speed** at the bottom (when frictionless). That's why energy methods beat kinematics here: you don't need to know the shape of the path or the angle at every instant. You just need the start and end heights.

steps.prompt: A **4.0 kg** ball and a **1.0 kg** ball are released from rest at the **same height, 2.5 m**, on frictionless ramps of totally different shapes. How fast is the **heavier 4.0 kg** ball moving at the bottom? (g = 9.8)
hints: Speed at the bottom is v = √(2gh). Does mass appear anywhere in that formula?
v = √(2 · 9.8 · 2.5) = √49 — the 4.0 kg is a red herring.
steps.solution:
Mass cancels out of mgh = ½mv², leaving v = √(2gh) = √(2 · 9.8 · 2.5) = √49 = **7.0 m/s**.

Both balls arrive at exactly the same speed — the 4.0 kg vs 1.0 kg and the different ramp shapes are deliberate distractions. Only the drop height matters.

steps.title: When friction joins in
steps.body:
Friction doesn't *destroy* energy — it converts mechanical energy into **thermal energy (E_th)** that heats the surfaces. The amount converted is just the friction force acting over the distance slid: E_th = F_f·d.

So the honest energy budget for a slide down a ramp is **everything in = everything out**:

steps.prompt: Before crunching numbers, nail the **signs**. A crate slides **down** a rough ramp. Drag a word into each blank — work is W = F·d·cosθ, so it's the **direction** of each force vs. the motion that sets the sign.
lines: **Friction** points {fricDir} the motion, so the work it does is {fricSign}.
The **normal force** points {normDir} the motion, so the work it does is {normSign}.
**Gravity** has a component pointing along the motion, so the work it does is {gravSign}.
Gravity pumps in more energy than friction drains, so the **net** work is positive — meaning the crate {motion}.
steps.solution:
Work is W = F·d·cosθ, where θ is the angle between the force and the motion — the **direction** decides the sign.

• **Friction** opposes the slide (θ = 180°, cos = −1), so it does **negative** work — draining kinetic energy into heat.
• The **normal force** is perpendicular to the motion (θ = 90°, cos = 0), so it does **zero** work.
• **Gravity's** along-slope component points the same way as the motion (θ < 90°, cos > 0), so it does **positive** work.

Add them: gravity adds more than friction removes, so the **net** work is positive. By the work–energy theorem (W_net = ΔKE), positive net work means kinetic energy rises — the crate **speeds up** as it descends, just not as fast as it would with no friction.
steps.hint: Same direction as the motion → positive work. Opposite → negative. Perpendicular (90°) → zero. Then net work sets whether KE rises or falls.

steps.prompt:
**Setup:** A **2.0 kg** sled starts from rest and slides down a **4.0 m**-long ramp, dropping a vertical height of **2.0 m**. Friction turns **10 J** of energy into heat.

**Part A —** How much gravitational PE does the sled have at the top? (g = 9.8)
hints: Use the vertical height, not the 4.0 m along the ramp.
steps.solution: Gravitational PE uses the vertical height: PE_g = mgh = 2.0 × 9.8 × 2.0 = **39.2 J**.

steps.prompt: **Part B —** How much kinetic energy does the **2.0 kg** sled have at the bottom, after friction takes its cut?
hints: Energy balances as PE_g = KE + E_th. Solve for KE.
steps.solution:
The energy balance is PE_g = KE + E_th, so KE = PE_g − E_th.

KE = 39.2 − 10 = **29.2 J**. (Without friction it would have been the full 39.2 J.)

steps.prompt: **Part C —** How fast is the **2.0 kg** sled moving at the bottom?
hints: The kinetic energy at the bottom sets the speed: ½mv² = KE.
steps.solution:
Kinetic energy gives the speed: ½mv² = KE.

v = √(2·KE / m) = √(2·29.2 / 2.0) = √29.2 ≈ **5.4 m/s**.

steps.prompt: **Part D —** The sled slid **4.0 m** along the ramp while losing 10 J to heat. What was the average friction force?
hints: Heat generated equals the friction force times the distance slid: E_th = F_f·d.
Solve for F_f: F_f = E_th / d = 10 / 4.0.
steps.solution:
The thermal energy is the friction force acting over the slide distance: E_th = F_f·d.

F_f = E_th / d = 10 / 4.0 = **2.5 N**.

steps.prompt:
**Challenge — chain three forms together.** A **1.0 kg** block is released from rest at the top of a **frictionless** ramp **2.0 m** tall, slides to the bottom, then runs along level ground into a spring.

**Part A —** How much kinetic energy does the block have at the bottom of the ramp? (g = 9.8)
hints: The ramp is frictionless, so all the gravitational PE becomes kinetic energy: KE = mgh.
steps.solution: With no friction on the ramp, every joule of gravitational PE turns into kinetic energy: KE = mgh = 1.0 × 9.8 × 2.0 = **19.6 J**.

steps.prompt: **Part B —** That **19.6 J** block now crosses a **rough patch** where friction does **5.0 J** of negative work, then slams into a spring and compresses it to a dead stop. How much elastic PE is stored in the spring at maximum compression?
hints: Follow the energy: the block's KE either heats the rough patch or ends up stored in the spring.
steps.solution:
Track every joule. The block starts the floor with 19.6 J of KE. Friction drains 5.0 J to heat, and the rest is stored in the spring at the instant the block stops:

PE_s = KE − E_th = 19.6 − 5.0 = **14.6 J**.

Three forms — gravitational PE → kinetic → elastic PE (with a friction tax along the way) — all bookkept with one conservation statement.

steps.title: Lesson complete!
steps.body:
You turned conservation of energy into a problem-solving engine: found speeds with v = √(2gh) (mass and path be damned), ran a full budget down a rough ramp, and chained gravity → motion → heat → a spring in one shot.

Last stop: how *fast* energy moves. Time for **power**.
